title: Form 5472 deadline calculator
summary: Calculate the regular due date and extension deadline for a Form 5472 filing package.
description: This calculator uses the Form 5472 and Form 7004 rules most relevant to foreign-owned U.S. disregarded entities and reporting corporations. It is designed to give founders a practical filing calendar, not legal advice.
formulas: Regular due date = 15th day of the 4th month after tax year end for the seeded corporate-return workflow.
Extension request due date = same as the regular due date.
Foreign-owned U.S. disregarded entity filing package = Form 5472 attached to a pro forma Form 1120.
caution: This seeded calculator follows the general Form 1120 timeline used by the public beta pages. Always verify the exact due date against the cited IRS instructions if the entity has a short year, a non-calendar year, or unusual facts.

title: California LLC calendar calculator
summary: Generate the main California LLC payment and return dates for a selected tax-year pattern.
description: This calculator turns California's annual tax, estimated fee, and return-due-date rules into a working compliance calendar for LLC owners.
formulas: Annual tax due date = 15th day of the 4th month of the current taxable year.
Estimated LLC fee due date = 15th day of the 6th month of the current taxable year.
Return due date varies by classification, including partnership-style and single-member rules.
caution: California LLC rules differ by entity classification and ownership. This tool is a practical date engine, not a substitute for classification analysis.

title: Texas franchise obligation checker
summary: Check whether a taxable entity is above the Texas no-tax-due threshold and estimate the seeded rate logic.
description: This calculator uses Texas's current public threshold and rate table for the 2026 and 2027 report years to help users understand whether they are in the no-tax-due zone or in the tax-computation zone.
formulas: 2026 and 2027 no-tax-due threshold = 2,650,000 USD total revenue.
Retail and wholesale rate = 0.375%.
Other taxable-entity rate = 0.75%.
caution: This checker simplifies the Texas franchise base and does not replace full margin analysis, E-Z computation eligibility review, or filing-form selection.

title: Switzerland VAT calculator
summary: Calculate Swiss VAT on a net-to-gross or gross-to-net basis using current federal rates.
description: This calculator applies the current Swiss VAT rate categories published by the Federal Tax Administration and lets users reverse-engineer net or gross invoice values.
formulas: Normal rate = 8.1%.
Reduced rate = 2.6%.
Special accommodation rate = 3.8%.
Gross to net formula = gross / (1 + rate).
caution: This tool calculates arithmetic only. It does not determine which Swiss VAT category legally applies to the supply.

title: UK Self Assessment late-filing calculator
summary: Estimate the late-filing penalty stack for a Self Assessment return.
description: This calculator applies the public HMRC late-filing penalty ladder for Self Assessment returns and keeps filing penalties separate from payment penalties.
formulas: Immediate late-filing penalty = 100 GBP.
After 3 months = 10 GBP per day for up to 90 days.
After 6 months = greater of 5% of tax due or 300 GBP.
After 12 months = another greater of 5% of tax due or 300 GBP.
caution: This calculator estimates filing penalties only. Late-payment penalties and interest may also apply.

title: Florida annual report cost calculator
summary: Estimate the filing cost of a Florida annual report by entity type and timing.
description: This calculator turns the Florida annual report fee table into a fast maintenance-cost estimate for LLCs, corporations, and certain partnerships.
formulas: LLC on-time filing fee = 138.75 USD.
Profit corporation on-time filing fee = 150 USD.
Not-for-profit corporation on-time filing fee = 61.25 USD.
LP and LLLP on-time filing fee = 500 USD.
Late fee after May 1 = 400 USD for LLCs, profit corporations, LPs, and LLLPs.
caution: This tool covers the filing-fee side only. It does not address any separate Florida corporate-income-tax consequences.
